entries.title: Afghanistan: Deadly flash floods hit Nuristan province
entries.summary: Some 20 people have been killed and more than 80 injured, with the city of Parun hit particularly hard. A lack of infrastructure and the effects of climate change have left the country vulnerable to natural disaster.
entries.content:
Afghanistan

Afghanistan: Deadly flash floods hit Nuristan province

July 21, 2026

Some 20 people have been killed and more than 80 injured, with the city of Parun hit particularly hard. A lack of infrastructure and the effects of climate change have left the country vulnerable to natural disaster.

Eastern Afghanistan was struck by flash floods that left at least 20 people dead, 80 injured, and over 100 missing, authorities confirmed late on Monday.

Yousaf Hammad of the National Disaster Management Authority said that a rescue mission was underway to locate the missing and bring them to safety if possible.

"Unfortunately, severe floods today in Parun city and several other areas of Nuristan province have caused heavy loss of life and property among our fellow citizens,” Hammad said, stressing that there had been severe damage to people's homes, farmland, and public facilities.

"We urge all citizens to stay away from flood-prone areas and zones at risk."

A spokesman for the Taliban government in Kabul added: "We pray for those who lost their lives in this incident and for a speedy recovery for the injured."

Why Afghanistan is vulnerable to floods

A variety of factors have left Afghanistan particularly vulnerable to extreme weather events. Decades of conflict, followed by the removal of foreign investment after the return of the Taliban in 2021 has left prevention infrastructure in poor condition, where it exists at all.

Deforestation, a struggling economy, and the effects of climate change have compounded these issues.

More heavy rain, thunderstorms, and extreme winds were predicted to continue in 10 of the countries 34 provinces in the coming days, Taliban officials said.

Edited by: Rana Taha

Don't let the algorithm hide the news. If you rely on our team for trusted reporting, please take a moment to select us as your Preferred Source on Google by clicking here and hitting the "star" or "preferred" button, so you'll always see our verified news first.

entries.title: How heat waves are messing with your sleep
entries.summary: Residents across Europe have struggled to sleep during the latest record heat waves. But as the planet continues to warm, sleep deprivation could create an epidemic of mental and physical ill-health.
entries.content:
July 21, 2026

Residents across Europe have struggled to sleep during the latest record heat waves. But as the planet continues to warm, sleep deprivation could create an epidemic of mental and physical ill-health.

During the June heat wave that brought record temperatures to western Europe, some 65% of adults in the UK struggled to sleep, according to a recent poll. Near half of those lost three or more hours sleep a night.

Sleep disruption is an inevitable consequence of longer and hotter summers fuelled by human-made climate change. As the fastest-warming continent that is heating at more than double the global average, Europe is a sleep loss hot spot.

In the German state of Saxony in June, a weather station reported a tropical overnight minimum temperature of 29.4 degrees Celsius (85 degrees Fahrenheit) .

Such overnight extremes are now 100 times more likely due to climate change caused primarily by burning fossil fuels, notes World Weather Attribution, a global scientific body analyzing how climate drives weather extremes.

Climate change disrupts sleep

Heat-driven sleep loss has only recently been recognized as an important indicator of how climate change affects both physical and mental health.

A global team of researchers collated 165 million nights of data from nearly 318,000 users of wearable and under-mattress sleep trackers to monitor the impact of rising temperatures on sleep. In a paper published in July,they showed that when nighttime temperatures rise from around 12°C to 27°C, people face about a 40% higher chance of sleeping less than six hours.

Humans can't maintain cognitive function, memory or emotional balance when they sleep less than seven hours a night, noted Ty Ferguson, a research fellow at Australia's Adelaide University who studies the potential health and social impacts of climate-driven sleep disturbance — but wasn't part of the July study.

In addition to impacting mood and attention, disrupted sleep can increase diabetes risk, depression, heart disease and even obesity, according to Ferguson.

'Sleep essential for immune function, mental health'

"Sleep is a fundamental physiological necessity essential for human immune function, mental health, and daily well-being," said Bowen Chu, a researcher at the School of Atmospheric Sciences at Nanjing University in China who co-authored a March study on the potential economic impacts of climate-driven sleep erosion in children.

Unlike traditional climate impact research focused often on extreme weather events, understanding sleep loss "allows scientists to quantify the gradual, day-to-day, and cumulative effects of ambient temperature rise on the everyday lives and health of global populations," Chu told DW.

"Temperatures are also rising faster at night than during the day," notes Kelton Minor, an associate professor at the University of Copenhagen in Denmark whose research team, along with the World Sleep Society, has called for a global sleep task force to better understand and prepare for sleep depletion in a hotter world.

Nights are getting hotter in part because clouds absorb heat and send it to earth after dark — unlike daytime when they reflect sunlight. Researchers have found that cloud patterns are changing in ways that amplify global warming.

'Quietly accumulating' sleep decline underreported

Sleep loss is probably underreported as a climate impact since it "doesn't produce a dramatic single event — no headline death toll, not as visible damage," noted Kim van Daalen, and environmental epidemiologist at the University of Cambridge and co-author of a recently published paper on "Sleep loss in a warming world."

"It accumulates quietly, night after night, which makes it easy to miss in coverage that tends to focus on acute heat wave deaths or hospitalizations," said van Daalen of the subtle impacts of sleep deficits.

"Heat-related sleep loss is likely to widen existing health and socioeconomic inequalities," said van Daalen. Children may struggle with concentration, learning and emotions, while "greater cardiovascular and metabolic strain" is being placed on older adults and people with chronic illnesses when sleeping less.

"Chronic sleep loss driven by high temperatures is associated with decreased labor productivity, reduced learning efficiency, potentially an increased risk of traffic accidents, and heightened long-term healthcare burdens on society," said Chu of the broader societal impacts of sleep interruption.

"Poor sleep makes it harder to work, learn, care for others and maintain good health," he said, adding that low-income households are more exposed because they live in poorly ventilated homes or cannot afford cooling.

Both lower income populations within wealthy nations, and residents of lower income countries, may be up to three times more vulnerable to the impact of heat on sleep, according to Minor.

This is explained by "lower access to air conditioning and other economic lifelines that can keep the heat out," he said, pointing to "houseless individuals trying to sleep on the streets, migrants in sweltering tents, incarcerated populations in searing hot prison cells, infants in scorching bedrooms, as well as pregnant and new mothers already struggling to sleep."

What are the solutions?

Even in a scenario where nations achieve net zero emissions, adaptation to climate-driven sleep loss will be an ongoing necessity since temperatures will take decades to fall, noted Ferguson.

Increased access to fans or air conditioning, lighter and breathable bedding, greater building insulation, and limiting heat-trapping concrete in cities are some of the ways climate-related sleep decline can be mitigated, he explained.

More green spaces and solar-radiation reflective surfaces along with passive cooling building designs that utilize window shades and improve ventilation, will be vital structural solutions, according to Chu.

Adjusting schedules to avoid work during extreme heat waves will also be fundamental to adaptation measures that help maintain sleep duration in a hotter world, he said.

These solutions will be especially important in European countries like Germany that traditionally have little air conditioning, for example, compared to the US or Australia, notes Minor.

"Adapting the approximately one third of our lives that we spend sleeping to nighttime warming already warrants European policy prioritization and adaptive innovation," he said.

Edited by: Sarah Steffen

entries.title: Germany news: Merz to host Azerbaijan President Aliyev
entries.summary: Talks between the German chancellor and Ilham Aliyev will focus on energy and Armenia. Meanwhile, Interior Minister Alexander Dobrindt will present a report on sexual offenses against children. DW has more.
entries.content:
Germany

Germany news: Merz to host Azerbaijan President Aliyev

Published July 21, 2026last updated July 21, 2026

Talks between the German chancellor and Ilham Aliyev will focus on energy and Armenia. Meanwhile, Interior Minister Alexander Dobrindt will present a report on sexual offenses against children. DW has more.

Skip next section What you need to know

What you need to know

  • Talks between Chancellor Friedrich Merz and the Azerbaijani leader are expected to focus on energy issues and the peace process with Armenia
  • A 2025 report on sexual crimes against minors in Germany is to be presented in Berlin
  • An anti-racism association is also to present an annual report on far-right, racist violence

Here is a round-up of the top headlines from and about Germany on Tuesday, July 21, 2026:

Tired of missing our real-time updates? Click here to add us as a Preferred Source on Google. Then tap the "Star" or "Preferred" to keep DW News at the top of your feed.

Skip next section Merz to sign agreement with Azerbaijan's Aliyev after talks

Merz to sign agreement with Azerbaijan's Aliyev after talks

Chancellor Friedrich Merz is to hold talks with Azerbaijan's president, Ilham Aliyev, in Berlin on Tuesday, with the focus expected to be on issues such as energy and trade in raw materials.

The talks will also touch on the peace process between Azerbaijan and Armenia amid remaining tensions over the Nagorno-Karabakh region and other territorial issues, the German government has said.

Azerbaijan derives most of its state revenue from the sale of oil and natural gas, including to the European Union.

Ahead of the talks, Aliyev will first be received by President Frank-Walter Steinmeier earlier in the morning, before being welcomed by Merz with full military honors.

Skip next section Welcome to our coverage

Welcome to our coverage

A hearty guten Tag from DW's newsroom in Bonn to all our readers!

Tuesday is shaping up to be a day of reports in Germany.

Interior Minister Alexander Dobrindt is to join Holger Munch, the president of the Federal Criminal Police Office (BKA), in presenting a 2025 review of the situation regarding sexual crimes against minors.

An association for victims of racist violence will also be presenting its annual report for 2025, which is expected to show an alarmingly high level of such offenses.

And the charity organization Caritas will also be giving its donation results for 2025 along with a review of current humanitarian crisis zones and the problems caused by dwindling financial resources.

Alongside all of this, Chancellor Merz will be hosting Azerbaijani leader Ilham Aliyev, one of the longest-serving presidents in Europe.

Read on here for more details on these and other stories.

entries.title: India's 'Cockroaches' reportedly cancel march but maintain protest
entries.summary: India's 'Cockroach' movement protesters have reclaimed their dismantled protest site, a day after police and a sea of youth protesters clashed in Delhi. Organizers however said they are canceling a march. Follow DW.
entries.content:
Published July 21, 2026last updated July 21, 2026

India's 'Cockroach' movement protesters have reclaimed their dismantled protest site, a day after police and a sea of youth protesters clashed in Delhi. Organizers however said they are canceling a march. Follow DW.

Skip next section What you need to know

What you need to know

  • Cockroach Janta Party founder Abhijeet Dipke has reportedly said at a press conference that his protest movement will not march to the parliament again
  • The decision is meant to protect protesters against alleged 'police brutality'
  • The CJP says it will continue its protest until India's education minister resigns
  • Scores of young people have made their way back to the Jantar Mantar protest site

Here are the latest developments from India on Tuesday, July 21, 2026:

Tired of missing our real-time updates? Click here to add us as a Preferred Source on Google. Then tap the "Star" or "Preferred" to keep DW News at the top of your feed.

Skip next section 'Cockroaches' leader Dipke apologizes to injured protesters

'Cockroaches' leader Dipke apologizes to injured protesters

Cockroach Janta Party founder Abhijeet Dipke on Tuesday visited a hospital in New Delhi to meet supporters who were injured during the attempted march to parliament.

In a message on X, he apologized to CJP supporters who he said were "brutally" beaten up by police personnel.

"I apologise to all our supporters, especially the girls who were brutally beaten by male police officers," Dipke wrote.

"We could have done better. I could have done better to protect you from the inhumane actions of the Delhi Police," he added.

The CJP head also claimed that a girl was in a critical condition after being injured in a police baton-charge that ensued on Monday as a protest march of thousands tried to push its way through security barricades.

There is no statement from the authorities on Dipke's claim.

Meanwhile, the CJP shared a form on social media asking people who had been detained and facing legal charges over the march to reach out.

"We will provide the necessary legal help," the movement said.

According to a police statement, 70 protesters were detained on Monday and a legal action was being initiated against them.

Ahead of the march on Monday, authorities had imposed a ban, barring the assembly of five or more people, except at the designated protest site of Jantar Mantar. The police had warned of legal action against violators of the order.

On Tuesday, CJP spokesperson Ashutosh Ranka called on the Delhi police to release the detainees.

"Delhi police needs to clearly tell how many people are still under detention from yesterday's protest," he said.

Skip next section Protesters return to protest site, Jantar Mantar

Protesters return to protest site, Jantar Mantar

Following the confrontation on Monday, CJP protesters made their way back to their Jantar Mantar protest site, where they had been holding a sit-in demonstration for the past month, to find it dismantled by the police.

On Tuesday morning, Dipke's message to supporters and Prime Minister Narendra Modi's government was: "We won't back off".

CJP spokesperson Ashutosh Ranka declared: "We won't stop. Pradhan has to be sacked."

As of this morning, at least 150-200 ⁠protesters have ​gathered at the protest site poised to protest amid a light drizzle.

"Jantar Mantar Is Again Packed! The more you suppress us, the more we will rise!!" CJP wrote on X.

Security around the protest site and the rest of central Delhi continues to remain tight.

Skip next section India's 'cockroach' party vows continued protest, parliament march reportedly called off

India's 'cockroach' party vows continued protest, parliament march reportedly called off

Reports on Tuesday cited India's Cockroach Janta founder Abhijeet Dipke as saying that the youth-led movement will not carry out a march to the parliament again because "the police ⁠will hurt the youth again".

The CJP has however vowed to continue its sit-in protest until its demand for the resignation of Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan is met.

"We had to call off the protest yesterday because we did not want more youth to ​be injured," news agency Reuters quoted CJP founder ⁠Abhijeet Dipke as telling a press conference.

"We will not march again because the police ⁠will hurt the youth again," ​he said.

There was no immediate statement from the CJP on the march on its official channels.

The reported decision to not march comes a day after some 180 people were reportedly injured as thousands of CJP supporters, attempting to get to the Indian parliament, clashed with the police.

According to Delhi Police figures, 118 police personnel and 60 protesters were injured in the confrontation that also saw authorities using batons and tear gas on the youth protesters.

The CJP alleges that the police "brutally" beat up the protesters while the Delhi police has criticized "unruly, aggressive and violent behavior" by a "violent mob".

Skip next section Welcome to our coverage

Welcome to our coverage

This is Dharvi Vaid Dhulia from DW’s studio in New Delhi, bringing you our daily wrap of all the biggest headlines creating a buzz in India.

India is still reeling from the scenes witnessed in the heart of the capital yesterday as thousands of youth protesters descended onto central Delhi, in what was one of the biggest demonstrations the city has witnessed in five years.

The outpour also marked one of the biggest public challenges to Prime Minister Narendra Modi in his three-term tenure.

The protesters were participating in a march to the parliament called for by the viral albeit newly-formed Cockroach Janta Party, seeking the resignation of the country’s education minister.

Authorities had banned the march and as the sea of protesters tried to push their way across a heavy security cordon, they were met with baton-charge and tear gas.

The CJP has alleged police brutality amid reports of several people being injured.

The march has now reportedly been called off, but India’s "cockroach" protest seems to be holding ground as the CJP says they will push ahead with their demands until Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan steps down.

Stay tuned as we bring you more.

entries.title: Tate brothers to fight extradition to UK, says lawyer
entries.summary: Andrew and Tristan Tate's lawyer said that the brothers are victims of a "political witch hunt." He has vowed to challenge their extradition to the UK.
entries.content:
July 21, 2026

Andrew and Tristan Tate's lawyer said that the brothers are victims of a "political witch hunt." He has vowed to challenge their extradition to the UK.

Controversial social media influencers Andrew Tate and his brother Tristan Tate will fight extradition to the United Kingdom, where the two are accused of rape and sex trafficking, their lawyer said Monday.

The brothers were arrested by the authorities at the weekend in Miami after prosecutors in the UK announced fresh charges, including further counts of rape, sex trafficking and assault.

The Tate brothers now face 59 charges in total — 42 against Andrew and 17 against Tristan.

In the coming weeks, US authorities will consider whether they meet the conditions for extradition to the UK.

What did the Tate brothers' lawyer say?

Speaking outside a court in Miami, their lawyer Joe McBride, said that the Tate brothers are victims of a "political witch hunt."

McBride argued his clients were being "attacked for what they say, not what they've done."

"We are, of course, objecting to extradition because Andrew and Tristan are innocent. They've never done nothing wrong. They shouldn't be extradited for crimes they did not commit," he said.

The brothers were surprised when they were arrested, McBride said, adding that the attempt to have them extradited was politically motivated. "There's no question about it," he told reporters.

He suggested their arrests "had a lot to do with" former British Prime Minister Keir Starmer's decision to step down, without providing any details.

McBride also said it was no coincidence that the brothers were arrested after meeting "friends in Washington, DC.”

"We went to see our Congress people in DC and now we're getting arrested. This feels political," McBride said, without specifying who the brothers met or what they discussed.

Andrew Tate is one of the most prominent faces of the "manosphere" and his content focuses on traditional masculinity, anti-feminism, and self-improvement.

He has over 10 million followers on X but has been banned from other platforms for violating hate speech guidelines, including YouTube, TikTok and Instagram. His misogynistic comments have been widely condemned.

Edited by: Rana Taha

Don't let the algorithm hide the news. If you rely on our team for trusted reporting, please take a moment to select us as your Preferred Source on Google by clicking here and hitting the "star" or "preferred" button, so you'll always see our verified news first.

entries.title: UK's new PM Andy Burnham cuts household electricity tax
entries.summary: The UK's new leader moved to remove VAT from electricity bills from October 1. The move comes before Burnham chairs his first cabinet meeting later today.
entries.content:
United Kingdom

UK's new PM Andy Burnham cuts household electricity tax

July 21, 2026

The UK's new leader moved to remove VAT from electricity bills from October 1. The move comes before Burnham chairs his first cabinet meeting later today.

Britain's new Prime Minister Andy Burnham on Tuesday announced that there would be a cut in taxes on household electricity bills, by removing VAT from October 1.

In a statement issued by Downing Street, the measure was described as an effort to "give millions of households breathing space this winter."

"We’re taking immediate action to cut taxes on energy bills, put more money in people’s pockets and bring back hope," Burnham said.

According to the government statement, cutting VAT on electricity bills was expected to shave off £45 (€52.97, $60,48) off the yearly price cap.

The statement went on to say that the measure was being funded through the cancellation of the Digital ID program that was going to cost £1.8 billion over the next three years.

Burnham to convene first cabinet meeting

Burnham is expected to set out his first policies as new British prime minister as he convenes his first cabinet meeting on Tuesday.

On Monday, as he took office, Burnham said he would present a 10-year plan for a new economic and political model by the end of the year.

He said he was aware that the UK is facing a cost of living crisis and promised to put forward a few proposals to ease the situation.

The Labour leader said his very first concern was to "end rough sleeping in this country" and alleviate the housing crisis, before promising to "bring back hope" to all UK residents.

Burnham has made some key changes to his administration. Ex-defence secretary John Healey replaces Rachel Reeves as finance minister, Ed Miliband becomes foreign secretary and left-wing figurehead Angela Rayner returns to her old post as housing secretary.

Rayner had resigned from her position as deputy prime minister and housing minister in September 2025, amid a property tax controversy.

Edited by: Rana Taha

Don't let the algorithm hide the news. If you rely on our team for trusted reporting, please take a moment to select us as your Preferred Source on Google by clicking here and hitting the "star" or "preferred" button, so you'll always see our verified news first.

entries.title: US imposes new 50% tariff on many Canadian goods
entries.summary: The tariffs will cover products including wine and hockey sticks, but not energy or fish. The move is the latest trade escalation between the US and its northern neighbor under the Trump administration.
entries.content:
July 21, 2026

The tariffs will cover products including wine and hockey sticks, but not energy or fish. The move is the latest trade escalation between the US and its northern neighbor under the Trump administration.

US President Donald Trump's administration on Monday announced it was imposing 50% tariffs on a wide range of Canadian goods. 

The White House said the tariffs would take effect in 30 days and cover "products ranging from wine to hockey sticks to cement."

Trump signed the three proclamations under Section 338 of the Tariff Act ​of 1930, a nearly century-old trade law that allows for tariffs of up to 50% on imports from ​specific countries.

However, the new tariffs will not apply to energy, potash (used in agricultural fertilizer), fish, critical minerals and goods already covered by existing tariffs under Section 232 of the law that relates to national security.

White House accuses Canada of 'discriminatory treatment'

The White House said the move was a response to Canada's "discriminatory treatment" of American goods.

Specifically, it said Canada imposed tariffs and quotas on US-manufactured cars that it did not apply to cars from other countries, and it said all but two Canadian states and territories have restricted the distribution of American alcohol.

"Over the past year and a half, only two countries have chosen to retaliate against President Trump’s tariffs rather than negotiate a deal with the United States: the People’s Republic of China and Canada," the White House said.

How did Canada respond to the new US tariff plan?

Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney said the US tariffs are "in direct violation" of the Canada-United States-Mexico Agreement (CUSMA), "the free trade agreement between Canada, the United States and Mexico."

Carney said Canada has made proposals to "modernize" CUSMA and "we stand ready to intensify those discussions in the coming weeks."

Ontario Premier Doug Ford condemned the proposed US tariffs, warning that they would have economic consequences on both sides of the border.

In a post on X, Ford vowed to continue advocating for Ontario's interests, arguing that Canada should match any US tariffs with equivalent countermeasures.

He said, “I'll never stop fighting to protect Ontario. If these tariffs proceed, Canada should respond tariff for tariff, dollar for dollar.”

Candace Laing, president and CEO of the Canadian Chamber of Commerce, described the Trump administration's latest tariff measures as "regrettable" in comments to AP news agency.

She urged both countries to use the 30-day period before they take effect "to make meaningful progress in advancing formal talks."

The implications of the tariffs extend beyond Canada, according to Scott Lincicome, vice president of general economics at the libertarian Cato Institute in Washington, D.C. He warned in comments also to AP that the measures would inject "massive uncertainty" into the global economy and affect a range of US trading partners.

"We crossed the Rubicon," Lincicome said, describing the use of Section 338 as "the nuclear option for Trump tariffs."

Wildfires add to growing US-Canada tensions

Trump has also suggested additional tariffs in response to Canadian wildfire smoke that has affected air quality in parts of the United States.

Although Trump and Carney attended the FIFA World Cup final together, US officials said their meeting was not intended to address trade issues.

The latest tariff measures reflect broader tensions between the two countries. Trump earlier in his second term mocked Canada and said it should be the 51st US state, sparking backlash from Canadians and pushback from Carney.

Edited by: Wesley Dockery

Don't let the algorithm hide the news. If you rely on our team for trusted reporting, please take a moment to select us as your Preferred Source on Google, so you'll always see our verified news first.

entries.title: Iran war: US launches fresh strikes as Trump vows revenge
entries.summary: The US has launched strikes on Iran for a 10th consecutive night as both sides vie for control of the Strait of Hormuz. US President Trump has promised revenge over the deaths of fallen American troops. DW has more.
entries.content:
Iran

Iran war: US launches fresh strikes as Trump vows revenge

Published July 20, 2026last updated July 21, 2026

The US has launched strikes on Iran for a 10th consecutive night as both sides vie for control of the Strait of Hormuz. US President Trump has promised revenge over the deaths of fallen American troops. DW has more.

Skip next section What you need to know

What you need to know

  • US launches 10th consecutive night of strikes on Iran
  • Iran says it targeted US base in Kuwait
  • Another tanker hit in Strait of Hormuz, UK Maritime Trade Agency says
  • Saudi Arabia condemns Houthis' Red Sea blockade

Below is a roundup of the main developments from the US-Iran war on July 21, 2026.

Tired of missing our real-time updates? Click here to add us as a Preferred Source on Google. Then tap the "Star" or "Preferred" to keep DW News at the top of your feed.

Skip next section Jordan targeted by Iranian drones

Jordan targeted by Iranian drones

Jordan's military said it had intercepted five drones launched by Iran.

The state-run Petra news agency reported that the interceptions took place during overnight attacks

The agency cited a senior military source as confirming that the interceptions took place without any casualties or damage caused on the ground.

Iran's Revolutionary Guard said their forces targeted a complex housing US forces, confirming the attack.

Jordan hosts US forces and aircraft and has therefore been a frequent target of Iranian attacks since a preliminary deal between Washington and Tehran collapsed some two weeks ago.

Skip next section Lebanon's military deploys in southern town after Israeli withdrawal, sources say

Lebanon's military deploys in southern town after Israeli withdrawal, sources say

Lebanese forces had started deploying in the southern town of Zawtar al-Gharbiyeh after Israel withdrew troops from the area, news agencies have reported, citing military sources.

The AFP news agency reported that Lebanese soldiers "have begun deploying to the village of Zawtar al-Gharbiyeh" in the Nabatieh region, after Israel occupied parts of southern Lebanon during their war with Hezbollah.

The move is in line with a plan sponsored by Washington that sees Lebanon's military assume greater responsibility for security and work to remove Iran-backed Hezbollah's weapons from the area.

According to the US-sponsored framework agreement between Lebanon and Israel, reached late June, Lebanon's armed forces will "gradually assume full and effective security responsibility in pilot zones, which will serve as the mechanism for phased and verified redeployments of the IDF (Israel Defense Forces) and the deployments of the LAF (Lebanese Armed Forces)."

Skip next section Iran's Revolutionary Guard claims attack on Amazon infrastructure in Bahrain

Iran's Revolutionary Guard claims attack on Amazon infrastructure in Bahrain

Iran's state media have reported that Iran's Revolutionary Guard (IRGC) claimed to have destroyed infrastructure belonging to US company Amazon in Bahrain.

IRNA reported that "multiple missiles" were launched against "Amazon's central data infrastructure in Bahrain, completely destroying it."

There has been no official confirmation from Bahrain and the claims cannot be independently verified.

Skip next section US military completes latest round of strikes on Iran

US military completes latest round of strikes on Iran

The US military said it completed another round of strikes against Iran, targeting military command centers, maritime capabilities, missile and drone launch sites, and air defense systems.

In a statement, US Central Command (CENTCOM) said the operation was aimed at degrading Iran's ability to continueattacks on commercial vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz, one of the world's most important maritime trade routes.

CENTCOM said commercial shipping through the waterway remained ongoing despite the conflict. According to the US military, its forces have helped facilitate the transit of approximately 900 commercial vessels and 450 million barrels of crude oil through the strait since early May.

The US military also said its forces remain positioned to respond to what it described as Iranian aggression against civilian shipping, pledging to hold Tehran accountable for attacks on vessels navigating the strategic waterway.

Skip next section Tanker hit in Strait of Hormuz, says UK Maritime Trade Operations agency

Tanker hit in Strait of Hormuz, says UK Maritime Trade Operations agency

A tanker was hit by an unidentified projectile in the Strait of Hormuz off the coast of Oman on Tuesday, according to the UK Maritime Trade Operations (UKMTO) agency.

The agency said it had received reports that a vessel was struck about 8 nautical miles (15 kilometers) northeast of Limah, Oman.

The incident comes a day after another ship caught fire following an attack in the same area, adding to concerns over maritime security in the strategic waterway. 

The strike occurred as tensions between Iran and the United States continued to escalate. Tehran has repeatedly insisted that ships use a route along Iran's coastline through the Strait of Hormuz, arguing that only the route designated by Iranian authorities is safe.

Meanwhile, the US military carried out a 10th consecutive night of strikes on targets in Iran, while Iranian media reported explosions near Qeshm Island and the southern city of Bandar Abbas.

Skip next section Iran says it targeted US base in Kuwait

Iran says it targeted US base in Kuwait

Iran’s military said it launched surface-to-surface missiles at Camp Arifjan, a US military base in Kuwait, in the early hours of Tuesday. According to state news agency IRNA, the strike targeted HIMARS missile systems operated by US forces at the base.

Iranian military public relations officials, quoted by IRNA said that "the army's ground forces targeted the HIMARS missile systems of the US terrorist army stationed at the Arifjan base in Kuwait with surface-to-surface missiles."

Iranian officials described HIMARS as "a mobile missile system with the ability to move quickly against ground targets" and said that targeting the system "causes damage to the offensive and defensive layers and reduces the enemy's missile power in aggressive crimes."

The announcement came as Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian said the conflict had developed into a "full-scale war," indicating a significant escalation in hostilities.

Camp Arifjan, located south of Kuwait City, hosts personnel from several branches of the US military, including the Air Force, Navy, Marine Corps and Coast Guard. The base serves as a key hub for US military operations in the region.

Skip next section Saudi Arabia slams Houthis' Red Sea blockade announcement

Saudi Arabia slams Houthis' Red Sea blockade announcement

Saudi Arabia has condemned the Iran-backed Houthis in Yemen after they announced a naval blockade in the southern part of the Red Sea.

With Iran and the US restricting ship movements in the Strait of Hormuz, Saudi Arabia had relied on its Red Sea ports to export oil.

Overnight, the Saudi Foreign Ministry expressed the "strongest condemnation of the allegations made by the so-called military spokesperson of the Houthi terrorist militia, who accused the kingdom of besieging the brotherly Yemeni people and claimed the imposition of a maritime blockade on the Kingdom."

The Houthis and Saudi Arabia have traded strikes in recent days after an Iranian plane attempted to land in Yemen's capital, Sanaa.

Skip next section US launches fresh strikes on Iran

US launches fresh strikes on Iran

The US has launched strikes on Iran for the 10th night in a row.

"The strikes are designed to further degrade Iranian military capabilities used to attack commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz," the US military's Central Command said on social media.

Iran's semi-official Mehr News Agency reported that air defenses were activated near the Bushehr Nuclear Power Plant on the southern coast. The complex has been hit by previous waves of US strikes.

Strikes between the US and Iran have escalated in an attempt to control the Strait of Hormuz.

On Friday, an Iranian strike on an air base in Jordan killed two US service members and left a third unaccounted for. Another US soldier was reported dead in Iraq on Saturday due to a "controlled detonation" of a downed Iranian drone. 

US President Donald Trump said on Monday that whenever Iran killed an American soldier, they would "pay for that killing many times over." 

Skip next section Welcome to our coverage

Welcome to our coverage

Welcome to our coverage of the US war with Iran.

The United States military has launched fresh strikes on Iran for a 10th consecutive night since US President Donald Trump declared on July 8 that the shaky ceasefire between the two countries was "over."

Stay tuned as we bring you the latest on the US conflict with Iran and other developments from the Middle East.

entries.title: Mexican drug lord ‘El Mayo’ sentenced to life without parole in US prison
entries.summary: Ismael "El Mayo" Zambada, a co-founder of the Sinaloa cartel and one of the world's most notorious drug traffickers, was sentenced to life in a US prison.
entries.content:
July 20, 2026

Ismael "El Mayo" Zambada, a co-founder of the Sinaloa cartel and one of the world's most notorious drug traffickers, was sentenced to life in a US prison.

Mexican drug trafficker Ismael "El Mayo" Zambada was sentenced to life in prison without the possibility of parole on Tuesday for co-founding and leading the notorious Sinaloa cartel.

Zambada was one of the most prominent Mexican drug traffickers to face sentencing in a United States court since Joaquin "El Chapo" Guzman was sentenced to life in prison in 2019.

"The almost unimaginable amount of drugs attributable to him and the number of murders that we can document is enough to justify Congress's decision to make this a mandatory life sentence," US District Judge Brian Cogan said.

The 76-year-old Zambada apologized "for the harm I caused and for the example I set," urging future generations to "choose a different path."

Zambada apologizes before sentencing

He was arrested in July 2024 after he said he was kidnapped in Mexico and flown to Texas.

Zambada helped found and lead the Sinaloa cartel, becoming one of the most wanted figures for US intelligence and law enforcement agencies for decades.

According to prosecutors, he ordered torture and plotted murders, including the killing of his own nephew, as the cartel flooded the US with tons of cocaine, fentanyl, methamphetamine and other drugs.

He pleaded guilty to charges that included engaging in a continuing criminal enterprise and racketeering conspiracy, acknowledging culpability for 85 underlying crimes. In exchange, prosecutors did not seek the death penalty.

"I grew up in an environment where violence and crime seemed normal, but now I know that's no excuse," Zambada said in court on Monday. "There comes a time when every person must take responsibility for their decisions. I didn't, and many people paid the price."

"El Chapo" partner joins cartel leaders serving life terms

Zambada's former partner "El Chapo" is already serving a life sentence in the United States.

He was convicted in 2019 and is serving a life sentence at the federal Supermax prison in Florence, Colorado.

Together they built the Sinaloa cartel into one of the world's most powerful drug trafficking organizations.

The Sinaloa cartel is one of six Mexican drug gangs that US President Donald Trump has designated as Foreign Terrorist Organizations.

Zambada was arrested with El Chapo's son, Joaquin Guzman Lopez, one of El Chapo's sons, who has pleaded guilty to federal drug charges. He has yet to be sentenced.

Zambada's arrest led to a splintering of the Sinaloa cartel, pitting his loyalists against backers of Guzman's sons, dubbed the Chapitos, or "little Chapos."

Edited by: Jenipher Camino Gonzalez

entries.title: AliExpress hit with record EU fine
entries.summary: The Chinese retailer has become the latest major online marketplace to face penalties under the EU’s Digital Services Act.
entries.content:
July 20, 2026

The Chinese retailer has become the latest major online marketplace to face penalties under the EU’s Digital Services Act.

AliExpress said it plans to appeal a penalty imposed by European Union (EU) regulators, who found shortcomings in the firm's efforts to curb illegal, unsafe and counterfeit goods.

The largest Chinese e-commerce platform in the EU, AliExpress, has been fined  €550 million ($629 million) for failing to tackle sales of illegal, unsafe and counterfeit products.

"The spread of counterfeit clothing, unsafe toys, dangerous cosmetics and other illegal and harmful products is not an unavoidable cost of shopping online - it is a failure by AliExpress to comply with its obligations under the Digital Services Act," European Commission Vice President Henna Virkkunen said on Monday.

It is the largest fine ever imposed under the Digital Services Act (DSA), which sets out clear rules for online services in the EU, targeting especially very large online platforms.

AliExpress said it will appeal the "disproportionate" fine, saying it considers the penalty excessive.

AliExpress faces October 20 deadline

The online retail giant, which belongs to the Alibaba Group, must pay the fine and has until October 20, to submit a plan to address the breaches. EU regulators will then review the plan, after which the company could face further penalties.

A year ago, AliExpress agreed to tighten controls on illegal and potentially dangerous goods, such as medicines and supplements. But the European Commission said the measures did not fully resolve its broader concerns.

The commission said AliExpress:

  • Failed in some cases to penalize traders selling illegal products, allowing them to continue operating on the platform.
  • Lacked sufficient staff to effectively review potentially illegal listings.
  • Inspectors faced excessive workloads, hampering oversight efforts.
  • Some illegal products were recommended or promoted before being removed from the platform.

But AliExpress said the EU's decision "ignores our sound risk management framework and the significant, proactive enhancements we have made."

The company also said it has worked with the Commission to meet its "evolving expectations."

DSA used to rein in Big Tech

According to the EU, AliExpress has 193 million users in the bloc, ahead of online fashion giant Shein and retailer Temu, which have 156 million and 130 million users, respectively.

"One in five Europeans say they shop once a month from Shein, Temu and AliExpress," Virkkunen said.

Temu was fined  €200 million in May, while Elon Musk's social media platform X was fined in December last year, both for DSA violations.

Edited by: Jenipher Camino Gonzalez
